title: 2026-05-28 2026 05 28 FEC
displayTitle: 2026-05-28 - Finance and Expenditure Committee - Reserve Bank of New Zealand, Monetary Policy Statement, May 2026
topic: Reserve Bank of New Zealand, Monetary Policy Statement, May 2026
description: Thursday 28 May 2026| Finance and Expenditure Committee Reserve Bank of New Zealand, Monetary Policy Statement, May 2026 Reserve Bank of New Zealand Dr Anna Breman, Governor Karen Silk, Assistant Governor (Money) Paul Conway, Chief Economist
descriptionSummary: Reserve Bank of New Zealand, Monetary Policy Statement, May 2026. Reserve Bank of New Zealand. Dr Anna Breman, Governor

chunks.text: Is there a normal guy that we have? Coming in? Yeah, from- Bright Yeah. Do you miss him? No. No, I prefer it. He was great. Want to go in there? Can you tell him exactly- Oops. Mr. Chair. Sorry. Thank you. Yeah. Still nice. That's nice. Morning.

Good morning, Governor. Morning, Governor. Good morning. Morning, Paul, Karen. Morning. Morning. This is the powerful threesome. Welcome. We're delighted to have you this morning, and we'll just open up for you to have some opening comments, and then obviously Q&A.

Yes. Well, thank you so much for having us here. It's really, really brief, but just to say that yesterday, the Monetary Policy Committee voted to leave the OCR on hold at 2.25%. Three committee members voted for the hold, and three voted to increase the OCR by 25 basis points, and I used my casting vote to hold.

I want to stress that this is the first monetary policy statement with attributed votes and names of the Monetary Policy Committee members in the record of meetings. Some more information on the judgements the different committee members did is in the record of meetings. And as a committee, we welcome this increased transparency, and we believe it will strengthen New Zealanders' understanding of monetary policy going forward.

Thank you. Thank you. And can I just acknowledge that it is actually... I know you led some of that work out in terms of that transparency, and I think it is helpful for New Zealand to see the tensions rather than just the decision. Thank you. We're going to kick off with Joanne De Beer Russell. Thank you, Chair. Good morning, and welcome. I think this is the first MPS you've fronted for us, is it, as monetary policy statement?

Second. Second one. This is the second one. February. I've lost track. And thank you for that transparency. I found the split vote really interesting because we're actually in very uncertain times, so I think that split vote reflects the sheer uncertainty we're trying to deal with. And I see it as a strength rather than a weakness that we have that degree of discussion within the Reserve Bank. So really welcome to that. Look, there's obvious issues around the war in Iran, but there's so much uncertainty there that actually what I really want to

focus on is some of the other issues, and that for me particularly is around employment and unemployment, and your projections there. So if I look at the difference between the February and the May monetary policy statements, we're now projecting quite a bit more unemployment than we were in February.

Can you just speak to that a little, please? Yes. So what we saw before the conflict in the Middle East was that employment was starting to grow, so we saw a recovery in the labor market. Now what we see is that we expect unemployment to stay at the current level or slightly higher, about 5.4%, into next year. And it's really the case, what's causing that is the weaker demand that comes from the fact that fuel prices have gone up and related products. And we see that that means that both households and businesses are more cautious in this

environment. We see that in the forward-looking indicators of different kinds of business surveys. It's what we hear when we go out and meet with businesses. We do expect growth to slow down in the near term, but we expect this year still to have some growth, and that means that going into next year when we see inflation falling back, that we'll see better growth and then that employment will start growing again.

Thank you. One of my understandings is that unemployment and inflation are inversely related. So if we're expecting unemployment to increase, we would expect inflation to more or less decrease. That's a way of controlling inflation rates. But we've obviously got this external factor of fuel prices fitting in here.

Is that something that's going on in this space? So what we do emphasize in the report is that this is a supply disruption and cost pushes that will increase some of the prices in the near term, and particularly then fuel prices and related prices. So we can have an environment where, unfortunately, inflation goes up and employment stays high for some time. But the fact that the labor market is weak in the near term, we think that that will mean that wage growth will be subdued, and that means that inflation is more likely to fall back towards

target relatively soon because of the weak labor market. There is a relationship, but unfortunately, it's complicated in the sense that we can have both higher inflation and in the near term, a weak labor market. Thank you. So I've got a couple more and then I'll- That's all right.

So what is always... Well, it hasn't always, it's something that puzzles me, is one of the things we're doing with the OCR, if we increase the OCR, mortgage rates go up, and that takes money out of circulation because it's going back to the banks. Now, at the moment, we've got fuel prices increasing, and that means that households have less money to spend on other stuff.

Yes. decreases. It's a way of taking money out of the economy as well. So this is why I'm puzzled about, I guess, this is why my instinct, I'm not an economist, would've been to hold the OCR because the money is being taken out of the economy in other directions. Are you able to...

It's a little bit of a lay person's understanding- Yeah... of what's going on. Yeah. So let me just start by saying that what we actually see is what you're describing. Of course, this is a hit to households' real income, and we do see, for example, in card transaction data that households are spending. It's too early to have the official statistics, but some early high-frequency data show that households are spending less on other things like retail and hospitality, for example.

What we do know is that businesses are facing higher costs, both for fuel but also for transport. And what we're looking for and discussing at quite some length in the monetary policy statement is how much will that weaker demand from higher costs, and higher inflation hold back other price increases.

And the more other price increases it can hold back, that means inflation will be passing relatively quickly and come back to a low and stable level. But we could also see firms being able to pass on these costs more, and then we see more broad-based inflation, and that's exactly what we will be watching really carefully now, and that would influence the OCR and where we're going.

But Paul, please- Yeah... comment as well Just to say, effectively, broadly speaking, there's two really large forces acting on medium-term inflation. One is what you described, which is weak demand in the economy because real income growth is weaker than it was. So that is taking inflation pressures out of the economy.

But on the other side of it, as the governor's talking about, as costs have gone up, the temptation for businesses is to pass that on in the form of higher prices. And if they were able to do that en masse, then that relative price shift and the price of oil would become generalized inflationary pressure.

So on the one hand, there's that going on, but then weak demand in the economy is pushing medium-term inflation in the other direction. And effectively our job is to thread an OCR through that so that on net, we end up at 2% inflation over the medium term. Thank you, and last question, Mr. Chair. Look, thank you.

I appreciate it's really uncertain times, but the other thing is in terms of those unemployment projections, have you factored in the announced public sector job cuts and the ongoing impact from those, not just the jobs, but also the downstream jobs as well? So the way that we do our forecasting is that we take official decisions into account, and this is well described in the monetary policy statement when we talk about the key projection assumptions.

And what we say there is that we use the half-year economic and fiscal update from 25, from December, and then we've updated that and adjusted for data revisions and some of the stronger than expected starting point in the December 25 quarter, the official statistics, while we await the publication of the budget, which will come out later today. So formally, we will take any new announcement into account when they've been formally decided on. So that is the formal way that we do the forecasting.

So currently, we are looking at... It's not been formally incorporated into this forecast. Oh, sorry. I need to follow up on that. So it could get worse. Unemployment could get worse. So we will consider everything that will affect employment going forward.

Yeah. We are seeing some parts of the New Zealand economy that is still performing well. For example, the agricultural sector- Mm-hmm... some of the manufacturing sector, some of the tech sector. There are other parts are performing weaker, especially small domestic firms that are dependent on the domestic economy.

And all of these factors we'll be taking into consideration, including the budget that will be presented later today, but not at this time. Thanks. We've just got a second- Thank you... from Chloe Swarbrick, and then we're going across to Dr. David Lewis. Thank you very much. I just wanted to pick up on that answer about the anticipated growth that RBNZ has forecast and thread the needle between a few points that are in the MPS.

So on page 20, RBNZ is saying that primary exporters' elevated payouts and revenues are being used to reduce debt, not to invest. On page 12, you say that commodity prices have assisted in the recovery and are expected to fall however. You also say that retail, hospitality, and tourism and residential construction firms are all reporting a weakening of demand. On page 51, you are saying that house prices are likely to decrease in 2026 and only gradually increase from 2027, and throughout the monetary policy statement you're saying that

businesses are cautious about investment, and unemployment is projected to be higher because of those businesses' caution in hiring new workers. So given that house price growth, continued commodity price increases, and business investment are all expected to dampen in the short to medium term, and that you have signaled today, or rather yesterday, that monetary conditions are likely to continue to tighten, basically we are going into a higher interest rate environment where both public and private sector investment

is being withdrawn. Where on earth is this growth going to come from? I think it is still important, and it is a very challenging environment to see that there are still parts of the economy that are performing really well. Mm-hmm. So for example, the agricultural sector is benefiting from high- Jenny, why don't you-... commodity prices in terms of meat prices, dairy prices.

We're seeing that the global supply chains and the ships are still coming into New Zealand so exporters can get their goods out. We're seeing that manufacturing is performing reasonably well. Mm-hmm. The forward-looking sentiment indicators are still okay in terms of those sectors.

We're also seeing that globally some people are looking forDifferent safe, reliable countries to source products from, and New Zealand is considered safe and reliable business partner. So there are still parts of the economy that are performing well. We're also stressing this in the record of the meetings.

We do say that we see regional and sectoral differences. Mm-hmm. And we actually saw that in the first quarter data and some of the recent numbers on export, that exports are performing better than expected. The New Zealand dollar has been almost unchanged since the beginning of the year, but since the conflict started, it has weakened a little bit, so that's also benefiting the exporting sector.

So there are clearly weaknesses, and we do say that this caution will dampen growth in the near term, but there's also some strong fundamental factors that means that when we get a bit more certainty, it's a globally uncertain environment. We do expect those investments to come back and household sentiment to also start increasing again. I don't know if Karen or Paul would like to add.

Can I, prior to the conflict- Mm... we were seeing growth in the economy. So we did have the point 2 in December last year was a wee bit lower than we were expecting. But all the forward-looking indicators were reasonably positive for Q1. We're projecting 1% growth in Q1. There's some seasonality there, but there was growth in this economy.

And yes, Q2 and Q3 this year, much like last year, after the tariffs are a bit weaker than we thought they were going to be, but then we do have growth returning from Q4 into next year. And the other factor there is low interest rates- Yeah... of course. We have cut the OCR significantly. Yes, we are heading into an environment where interest rates are likely to be a bit higher, because we've had this inflationary supply shock come at us again, unfortunately.

Dr David Watson. Yeah. You're next. Thank you for those comments. And completely understand the theme for around supply shop. Very encouraging to hear that ag, manufacturing, exports, and employment are just on the up- Producing a good scrap down... seeing through what's going on. Didn't say unemployment, said employment.

No, I said ag export commodity prices anyway. Yeah. So given that we have from that August publishing company, The Herald, this morning an article that says Iran sees a low possibility of a return to war and that opening the Straits of Hormuz will cause things to change, and I understand that it already has, and excuse the hyperbole, but oil prices have tumbled- Mm-hmm... and markets have reacted in an upward way.

So your prediction around near-term inflation's expected to increase and economic growth to weaken, given that information, and if that was to continue, do you still believe that growth will weaken? So we believe that growth will start picking up from the later this year, but I think it's really important to say that the outlook for New Zealand will depend on what happens globally, and that's why in this monetary policy- Mm... statement we have, in addition to our central projections, we do have alternative scenarios.

And some of those scenarios look at- It might be... because we want to emphasize that the main projection is based on what we have currently, the information is based on a view of the oil price and what that means for fuel prices in New Zealand, that is consistent with the futures curve, as you say it, where market is pricing oil going forward. We know that that has been very volatile over the past few months. So we do different scenarios where we do one scenario where we say, "Well, what if oil price increase even

further and stays high for longer? What would that mean for inflation and the New Zealand economy?" We also do another scenario where we look at what if we see considerably weaker growth, and then we discuss what could happen with monetary policy in these kind of scenarios. Just to emphasize that it is an uncertain environment, and we will have to adjust because our focus is to ensure that this temporary near-term higher inflation does not become embedded in the economy.

Yeah. So we will adjust- Mm... according to what happens globally and how that affect New Zealand. And we will set monetary policy according to what is best for New Zealand. Now, supplementary on that, because this is material to what we've just been talking about. You're saying that wholesale interest rates passing through to higher fixed-term mortgage rates- Mm... and to a lesser extent, term deposits.

So I'm interested, given that information, whether that's actually correct, and whether the market will react to that here as they have overseas. But also, I'm really interested in your reflection on these interest margins. Are we still paying too much for our mortgages?

So I will give you a relatively technical answer. Good. And that is that- It's your finance... we do see that what we call financial conditions have tightened. Yeah. And what we've seen that wholesale interest rates have gone up considerably, but also actual mortgage rates. Mm. So households are already facing higher mortgage rates without the OCR going up.

Mm. And that's also, we think, most like we don't have as good data on the interest rates that businesses are facing, but it's likely given wholesale interest rate gone up, the businesses are already facing higher interest rates as well. And when we look at the data, it seems that the term deposits rate have not been increased as much as mortgage rates and the interest rates that businesses face.

So that is tighter financial condition. We do think that that is dampening demand somewhat, but also then the medium-term inflationary pressures. But I'd like to hand over to Karen to comment a bit more on this as well. Yeah, I'll just add a little bit to that. So yes, mortgage rates have gone up in response to wholesale rates, which are marketAgain, reacting to market interpretation around what central banks will do in the face of higher inflation over the coming months.

The transfer has not been as strong into savings, in terms of the pass through there. There are a number of things that you think about in terms of the transmission there is, it's largely banks here in New Zealand that hold those- Mm... savings. They're also taking into account credit demand, which is not strong, and in a more cautionary environment isn't going to be strong. So therefore, what is the demand for liquidity when you're already sitting on a highly liquid position? And bank liquidity is very strong at this

point in time. And so that's why you don't see as much price transfer running through at this point in time. But we would expect over time that that will change and that you would see migration coming through into retail interest savings rates or term deposits anyway, as well as the transfer into mortgage rates.

But there's definitely a lag at this point in time, and that does increase the net interest margin, as you've said. Yeah. And so is that still translating into us paying too much for our mortgages as compared to other jurisdictions? Oh, relative to other jurisdictions, it is a higher margin. Yeah.

Thank you. There is more information in the Financial Stability Report on this kind of topics. But today we're focusing on the monetary policy. And a great banking inquiry. Claros will run. So on page 28, you note that natural gas prices in Asia and Europe have increased by 60 to 90%.

And you say, and I quote, "New Zealand does not import natural gas and is therefore not directly exposed to this price increase, but many of our trading partners in Europe and Asia will be affected." Am I interpreting that correctly as follows that basically, if the government's plan to establish a new LNG import facility is successful, that we will be opening ourselves up to yet another source of imported inflation, which makes your job of maintaining price stability all the more difficult?

I think that what I said before in terms of making the forecast based on current assumptions and current information is what we're focusing on. And so what we're looking at now is how does the conflict in the Middle East translate- Mm-hmm... into higher fuel prices in New Zealand currently.

So that is really- So- The focus on the current monetary policy statement If I may, however, on that point, am I to read between the lines correctly in that answer, Governor, that the RBNZ is not anticipating that this LNG import facility is going to happen? So I think there's nothing to read in between the lines. What I'm saying is that we're making the forecast based on the information that we have currently and what will happen, and when we get more information, we'll take that into consideration in our forecast.

So just to be really clear about what that information is that RBNZ is currently taking into account with its forecasting and its decision-making, what, in your mind, is the status of the creation of this billion-dollar LNG import facility and the potential ramifications for importer inflation and exposure to it?

So currently, what we're taking into consideration is that we will be looking for the information to come out, and when we have more information- Mm... then will we take that into the forecast. And currently, we don't have full information here exactly on how it will work out. So it's currently not effectively in your forecast with regard to our exposure to imported inflation.

Thank you for making that clear. So on page 17, you're saying, and I quote again, "Elevated non-tradable inflation prior to the conflict mostly reflects high administered price and energy inflation." So now in the middle of the conflict, that phenomenon is of course heightened. And throughout the monetary policy statement, the bank charts the relationship between reliance on imported energy, as we've been talking about, and our exposure to inflation. So I want to ask about this in reference to some of the comments made in April of this year by the European

Central Bank's board member, Frank Elderson, who said, and I quote, "That repeated energy price shocks make maintaining price stability increasingly difficult." And that meeting clean energy targets would weaken the link between volatile global markets and domestic prices. So what is the bank's view on price stability risks of increasing our country's reliance on imported fossil fuels?

So I think that what we are seeing is that global volatility will affect small open economies considerably. That's true for all small open economies throughout Asia and throughout Europe as well currently. Our job is very focused on ensuring that we meet the medium-term inflation target.

So if there is more volatility globally because of, it's the COVID shock or it's energy prices, it could be something else that will affect New Zealand, we will consider that, but we will stay focused on our mandate, and that is to bring inflation back in the medium term, considering also to not cause unnecessary volatility in economic output.

Mm. And I understand, of course, there are very limited tools at your disposal, which we've gone through many times over the last three years, especially throughout COVID. On page 23, you talk about how businesses are reporting that imported goods are increasing in price, and that they are reporting that other costs have continued to rise, including electricity, insurance, and council rates, which of course- Mm... information we can get from other sources.

What levers-Just to make it really clear to the, I don't know, half dozen members of the public who may potentially be tuned in, what levers does RBNZ have available to it to actually reduce these rising costs? And what levers does fiscal policy have available to it to reduce rising costs in electricity and insurance, if any?

No, but it's important that OCR and hiking or lowering interest rate is effective in terms of controlling medium-term inflation. Yeah. Then we sometimes have graphs that shows that the effect on some types of pricer is higher than on others.

Mm-hmm. So of course, there are certain things like global oil prices the OCR in New Zealand will not affect, or certain other prices are more external factors. But there is, if you look over time, the OCR, but it does take a bit of time before to be efficient. It is a good tool in terms of controlling medium-term inflation.

But we're not trying to claim that we can control all prices, but we target the overall price increases and not specific different kinds of price categories. Mm-hmm. So the target is really to get the overall level inflation to be around 2% over the medium term.

Mm-hmm. And the OCR is efficient. Mm-hmm. It's not perfect in any way, but it is efficient in reaching that. But we are very open and frank, and we've shown graphs showing that there are certain prices where we have more of an impact than others. Yeah. So just, I guess, to put that in crystal clarity, you guys don't have control over imported inflation, but your major tools effectively are to contract spending in the economy, which has flow-through to different effect on certain types of goods and services.

Well, even imported inflation, it's important to remember that how the firms that import set their prices- Mm-hmm... they control, and that's also affected by the OCR and the level of the OCR. So even imported prices to some extent was affected by monetary policy in New Zealand.

They do, yeah. And also there is a channel with both expectations, but also the exchange rate. Yes. So an higher OCR tends to strengthen the exchange rate, and that reduces imported inflation. Yeah. So yes, as I said, there are certain prices that we affect more than others, but even some parts of imported prices will be affected by monetary policy.

Thanks. We're going to go to Dr. Megan Woods. Thank you. And look, just following on from that very quickly, one of the things that I thought was really interesting in the report I noted the bit around LNG prices. You tracked what you saw as the trajectory for potentially, who knows in this world, in terms of crude and refined products over the coming months.

Have you seen similar analysis on what might happen with LNG? You're saying that we're likely to see sort of a residual 30% left in some scenarios in terms of the cost of oil. Are we expecting the same in regard to LNG? We have not done that analysis yet.

Okay, you haven't done that analysis. Right. Thank you. But following on from there, I thought this graph was really interesting. This is the fuel intensity over time. Yeah. Mm-hmm. So one of the things that we're seeing is a slight uptick in terms of the barrel of oil consumed per million dollars of GDP.

In terms of what we need to do to get this graph back onto the trajectory it was on, what would the impact on inflation be if New Zealand was to become less and fuel intensive? So I can answer in terms of how this oil shock affect consumer prices. We have to consider that fuel in the New Zealand economy that somehow affects inflation comes both, of course, from petrol.

So most cars are running on petrol, not diesel. Diesel prices is also affected, of course. I'm stating the obvious now. Diesel is really important for transportation, it's very important for agriculture. But what we measure is the consumer prices. Yeah.

So the petrol prices is more important for the direct effect of this crisis on inflation. But we also have to consider the fact that diesel costs have also gone up, but that's used by businesses mainly. Yeah. And then they face higher cost, and to some extent, that higher cost may be passed on to other businesses and then maybe to consumers.

Yeah. Sorry, my throat. Now I'm fine. Currently what we're seeing in inflation so far is just the direct effect on petrol prices. And over coming months, we will also focus on and get more information to the extent that firms are facing higher costs for transportation, how much they're passing on to consumers. But that's just to explain a little bit how we analyze these factors, and pointing out that for the direct effects on the consumers- Yeah... it is currently the petrol price.

We don't ignore the fact that other fuel costs are also going up. But then you have to consider that even plastics, which is used by many firms, will also go up because it's also based on petrochemicals. Fertilizers will also affect agriculture over time. So what we are saying is that we are aware of the fact that this will take some time before we see all of the effects, and then it will depend on how quickly global oil prices and peace talks evolve over the coming weeks and months.

And going forward- And the changes that you're- Suggesting or the question you're asking depends on what the cost of the substitute is. Yeah. Totally. So, and/or is it delivered through less demand? So there's a lot of dynamics you have to take into account when you're thinking about answering a question like that, because it's not just about oil in itself, it's what are you substituting to and/or what's happening to demand.

So our domestic- So that it is substitution, not contraction. And then what's the flow-on impact to GDP of that. Yeah. Yeah. Just the other question I just want to touch on is the transparency change, because I think this is huge, and congratulations on that. And just thinking- Especially in the age of social media.

Just thinking as we go forward, we had the split decision, and I think there's a very small subset of people in the country that are going to be absolutely obsessed with the different decisions. As you think about this into the future, we had the kind of three-three split with the Reserve Bank and the non-Reserve Bank decision makers, if I can use those terms. What are you going to do to kind of make sure, and I'm not accusing you of this, but I'm just interested in it, that we don't have a central RBNZ view and then the others.

What are we going to do to avoid the bump into conversations of those people that work together, kind of having a consolidated view versus the externals? It's a really important and good question, and I really think you should see everyone as Reserve Bank decision-makers. Mm-hmm. Because they all are part of, obviously, the Monetary Policy Committee, and they feel very strongly-... about their role. And everyone was in favor of increasing the transparency.

I think it's an important fact that we all get the same information. We all have access to the same team that present all the recent data, the economic forecast. I am very conscious when we have our meetings to ensure that everyone gets an equal amount of time to speak. And when we do the deliberations on monetary policy and everyone is free to talk about their view of it, of course. But I always speak last because I think it's important for the other people to give their opinions first, and that everyone gets an equal amount of time, so that

we do get equal weight across the different members of the Monetary Policy Committee. And the new charter also encourages the external members to take interviews and to do outreach, to give a speech if they want to. So I think that we will also get the opportunity to hear more directly from them going forward.

Well, look, congratulations. Thank you. It's a necessary and courageous way of doing things, and thank you for doing it. And I think it's going to be interesting to watch how it evolves over time. Thank you. Thanks, Megan. Nancy Lou. Thank you, Chair. Welcome, Governor. Thank you. I wanted to also add on to my congratulations to you for making that very transparent.

And it sounds like you are encouraging everybody to take on the equal weighting, sharing that responsibility. But I particularly am a big fan of when you shared earlier, you want to make sure that there is increased communication and transparency so New Zealanders can understand. I don't think it's necessarily about the Reserve Bank understanding each other or the committee members on this committee, but it is about the New Zealanders.

And the point that I want to reflect on is particularly where we're now under huge global uncertainty and it's unprecedented, right? It's global, so all countries equally are being impacted by the Iran war. And all countries and economies are also under the word of uncertainty. So my two questions, one relating to what you have summarized on page 20, which is the business and the sector bit. You mentioned in your earlier meetings when you came in front of us that you are keen to travel around the country, keen to go to the different sectors.

But I want more from you about what you're hearing from the sectors about this uncertainty. How uncertain are they? And what do they want to see, and how much are they absorbing it? That's number one part of the question. The other part is also around communication, which is how much are you communicating with the other jurisdictions, with the other reserve banks around the uncertainty, particularly in our part of the world, and with Australia? I would love to hear more about that. Thank you.

Yeah. Well, thank you so much. So we do outreach towards businesses in several different ways. So we do regularly business calls. So staff actually meets with a number of firms on a regular basis just to collect information and get a bit more a sense of where things are heading before we get it in the official statistics.

And then in addition to that, we also do it in person. I do outreach. Karen is traveling to Tauranga soon. I'm traveling to Napier tonight. Our external member is Healy Gourley, who's here. She joined me in Hamilton about a month ago. I know that Carl is also planning to join me on one of my business visits later. Currently, what we're hearing is-Actually, some businesses, especially those focused on exports, are doing well, but they are concerned about the uncertainty in the global environment.

Mm-hmm. The domestic firms that are more reliant on consumers, for example, they are struggling more in this environment. And I still think that many of them are surprisingly optimistic. But I think also because there's been a number of global shocks over the past few years, they've also seen that if they keep on focusing over the coming few years, some of them can keep investing, even in a difficult environment. They know they're better positioned.

Some of them have been very successful in terms of diversifying their exports. That's good. And that means that they're well-positioned in this environment. But if you look at the smaller businesses, hospitality businesses, depending on households that are being squeezed right now, that's where we see the toughest situation, because they've already been struggling for a number of years. The other question was on the international side, and- Yeah... both the Reserve Bank as a group and me personally have a lot of contacts with other central banks.

So I travel regularly to the Bank of International Settlements that's headquartered in Basel in Switzerland, and that is about 68 central bank governors who meet six times per year. One is digital, and the other five meetings are actually in person. So I was there in early May. I was there in March.

I also went to the IMF meetings to get a chance. We also have an East Asia-Pacific special collaboration between central banks called EMEAP. Karen was recently- Yeah... at the deputy meetings. I'm going to the governors meeting in Singapore in July. But I thought maybe you wanted to, since you were recently there, you might comment.

Yeah. So we have these twice yearly. And meet to discuss what's happening in our respective economies, what's happening across the region. They are- Mm... and you'll appreciate we have some emerging economies, we have some advanced economies within the group. So it's quite different in terms of the experiences, and starting point matters in these kind of situations.

Our trading partners in that part of the world have been doing very well over recent years. AI investment, broader technology investment has really created good positions for them. They continue to see that as a significant opportunity and buffers for their economies moving forward.

But likewise, they're also facing into significant issues as a consequence of oil prices because they are very oil-intensive and fossil fuel-intensive economies, and are very conscious of that as well, and expressing a real need for investment for diversification into greater renewables as a consequence.

Thank you. We're just going a supplementary on- Yeah. Just you mentioned about the uncertainty you are seeing from businesses and their confidence to invest. Is it all driven internationally price shock, or are there regulatory settings here in New Zealand that's probably giving cause for concern or confidence to go forward with investments?

You want to- Yeah. What businesses like is certainty. And they need certainty in order to be able to do the investment, take on extra debt, and/or cost of equity injections in order to be able to do that. I think if you went to various different sectors, they would argue that regulation doesn't make it as easy for them to do that. But at the moment, a lot of that precautionary behavior is a consequence of the repeat supply shocks that we see. It's not just about oil.

Yeah. It is about what happened last year with tariffs. Yeah. It is about what happened through COVID. So certainty is the biggest thing. Yeah. Dr. David, do you have a question? The domestic regulatory environment is fundamental- Yeah... in providing businesses with uncertainty. It's not all just an oil shock. No. It is at the moment. Yeah.

That's the predominant kind of force. But yeah, predictability- It is... and domestic regulatory settings is important. Yeah, a supplementary. Dr. David. Thank you, Chair. And thank you, Nancy, for your question. You'll notice that we hang on every word you say here, and the couple of words you used were diversifying exports. Is that markets or products or both?

So what I hear, I was thinking mainly about markets actually. So that's what I heard. I would be surprised if it's not products as well. But what we've seen, and I met a number of firms that talked about that they've been very actively over the past few years seeing many global shocks, been focusing on having lots of different markets to export to, and that they're also saying that they're now seeing the benefits of having done that in terms of being able to still see good demand for their products.

It's not- But some of them are also very skilled in terms of seeing where is demand in the global economy. So globally, there's a lot of, for sad reasons unfortunately, investments in defense spending. So some people that's coming from the agricultural business originally, they're moving more into security, and then they see a lot of demand, and that is benefiting them. So businesses, many have been skillful in diversifying both in terms of products and markets.

And diversity brings resilience. Yeah. Tom Rutherford. Great. I want to talk about the split decision yesterday. Obviously, the first time the committee has reported onThe individual voting of the members. In front of us, we have the three members who voted to keep. Oh, yeah. Okay. So that irony isn't lost in that sense.

I guess I'm keen firstly to understand the rationale from you three as to why you voted to stay. It's obviously evident in here. Mm-hmm. But I want to hear it from the horse's mouth. And then I would welcome those that are also here who voted to increase- This is the time to-... to share their views as well.

Yeah. So I think it is welcome that we now have the record of the meetings with names, because I think everyone felt more comfortable that they got to state their individual views in the record of the meetings. We have agreed, yesterday at the press conference and also at the hearings, that we will represent the Monetary Policy Committee, but I know that Carl has already given an interview, and there will be more interviews for the individual members so they can give their view.

What we have said yesterday is that the members who were in favor of raising the OCR, they were more concerned with the risk of cost pressure leading to stronger second-round effects, so that there'll be more persistence in inflation going forward. Those of us who voted for a hold, when we looked at the data and what that means for inflation going forward, we maybe put some more emphasis on the fact that core inflation is still very much within the band. It was 2.3% in the latest numbers.

Wage growth is still subdued and expected to be subdued, and medium and long-term inflation expectations are still well anchored. And in addition, we've seen this tightening in financial conditions. So mortgage rates, for example, have already gone up. Mm. Plus, we see the weak demand putting less risk on the medium-term inflationary pressures.

But we all say that we see the inflationary pressures and we all expect the OCR to need to be at a higher level going forward, and that we will hike at coming meetings. Great, and I'd love to hear from Paul and- Hayley... and Hayley. Sorry Karen. Karen.

No, from the members sitting behind you who voted. But we have made the agreement that at the press conference at today's meeting- Mm-hmm... we would represent the committee, and then we refer to the record of the meetings. Okay. And also to upcoming interviews for them to give more of their view. But we have actually discussed, so this is for the committee to consider, whether you want to have a longer table.

Yeah. I'm- That would allow all of us to be up here and answer questions at the same time going forward. But maybe Karen and Paul would like to add as well. Look, just quickly, I think it's important to emphasize what we agreed on- Yeah... which was the economic projections we did and the OCR track. The only difference was whether or not to increase by 25 or not yesterday, and we come back together in six weeks- Yeah... as of yesterday, and we will start afresh then. So, there's a difference of view across committee members

on that aspect of the decision, but there is no division in the committee. Our deliberations were robust and rigorous and respectful, and we all left that room with our mana in good shape. Mm. Oh, I don't think there't any diminishing from this side that the reporting of the voting and who voted what way is not a good thing.

Mm-hmm. That is a great thing to see. I guess the clarity I'm trying to seek is we're hearing from the three who voted- This is just to confirm... to keep the status quo. It's well laid out in the record of the meeting. Yeah. But I'm interested to know also from those that voted to increase- Yeah... you're saying they're going to do interviews and speeches and things. Can you step that through for us? What are they?

Well, hang on. Sorry. Yeah. Debra, it's okay. I think it's been asked and answered, so I think we'll leave that question there. I am mindful of time. I'm going to go to you for a quick primary, Debra. Thank you. I want to go- I know there's agitation. I have tried to be fair. Yeah, I know. You're doing really well. Look, I just want to go to the spare capacity. We've discussed a fair amount about the labor market, but there's a bit about non-labor market spare capacity- Mm-hmm... in the economy. You used something you call the

structural vector autoregression. Could we do that again next time? But could you just speak a little to what's going on with that spare non-labor capacity in the economy? Yeah. So when we talk about where the labor market is and the potential to increase employment, but then it's also the fact that businesses a certain level of capacity in terms of how much their machinery is being used, how much their factories are being used, and that kind of spare capacity, and we still believe that there are some.

It means that if demand increases, they can increase output relatively easily. In some cases, they're really constrained. They're operating at full capacity, and then they would have to buy new machinery to just increase output, and we're not at that point right now.

So we see the potential to, if we see stronger demand, to actually see stronger output relatively quickly as well. Okay. Are you seeing differences across sectors in that spare capacity- Yeah... I believe you said? Yeah. So there are definitely differences across sectors currently.

Mm. I would say that some sectors, say for example, hospitality. That's also very seasonal. Yeah. But now we're currently seeing households being a bit more cautious, and that means that they would have more spare capacity in terms of meeting demand- Mm... if demand comes back. So there's definitely sectoral differences.

Thank you so much, Debra. Thank you. Thank you. Governor, good to have you here. Obviously budget day today as well. But the finance minister has indicated thatwants to get bend the surplus, sorry, bend the deficit back down and get us back into surplus, likely to not see a huge amount of spending through the operating allowance today.

Will that have an upward or downward impact on inflationary pressures? That's- So what we- Good question, but hard Yeah. So, but this is important because- Yeah... as I said, the current main forecast is based on information that has not been provided yet.

So we will take that into consideration later, and will have to do a thorough review of all that information. We do think, and that was the discussion we had at the committee, that the global forces and what's happening to supply chains, oil prices, and how that plays through is such a large factor that is likely to have a larger impact on inflationary pressures going forward, compared to what we're expecting from the budget today, because quite a lot is already known.

So I think that's also why we have the alternative scenarios, just to emphasize how much the global factors are impacting. But it, of course, then how that plays out in the New Zealand economy. If we get our response to the oil shock right, we'll be in a good place to deal with any variance between what was in the Half yearly financial statement, which is the numbers that are in there now, compared to the budget numbers that we'll get this afternoon.

No, I want you- I'm not quite clear that you did talk about medium term inflationary pressures. So if the government was to surprise you and borrow and spend considerably more, would that have upward impact on OCR and potentially the amount that New Zealand households have to pay through their mortgage interest rates? So I would say that what would matter is we would always look really carefully at all the different detail to see if there are any inflation or deflationary pressures, and I don't want to speculate before we have the full picture on

that. But currently, we don't see inflationary forces from the fiscal side. Thank you. Chloe? Depends what you spend on. You raised the specter earlier of AI, and I just actually wanted to offer you an opportunity to provide some potential caveats or more information. Because if someone was to take the comment you provided before and run with it, it sounds like AI is the silver bullet.

It means that other countries are becoming more productive and more inclined to grow. However, we also know that AI, if you look at commentary and economists internationally, possibly a bubble, and is promising a lot of things that it cannot potentially deliver. Also comes with massive externalities in terms of the impact on the environment and the displacement of workers.

And I think we all know that when we have these gains in productivity from certain types of tools or technology coming online that might result in growth or productivity, but that distributional impacts mean that that is not necessarily evenly felt. So do you want to offer any greater detail to that statement that you made about how AI is an advantage?

I think that if we made comments on AI that are not balanced, we will review that. Yeah. I think that we see many different aspects, and we talk about monetary policy today- Yes... but we did talk about it in the Financial Stability Report in terms of risks, because we also see the risk that AI being used for cyber attacks and things like that, and we're very vigilant for those kinds of risks.

What we said in this report is that we know, and that's what Karen was talking about as well, that some of our Asian trading partners have seen an AI investment boom. And that has meant that growth is stronger and that is benefiting some of our exporters. But of course, we also see other sides in terms of how it will affect the labor market going forward. So, we believe it's one of the things that we are looking at from a broad range of perspectives.

And we definitely want to be balanced. Thank you, Governor. We have just about met time. Is there a final comment or commentary you'd like to leave to New Zealanders, obviously in the midst of this anxiety-induced budget day, what would be your parting comments? I want New Zealanders to feel that we're fully focused on bringing inflation back to target.

We do consider all the risks to the economic development. So our focus is on bringing inflation back to target without causing unnecessary volatility in economic output. Thank you all so much for your time. And we wish you all the best in the next few months. Thank you. Thank you. That was really useful.
Thanks, members. We'll see you at 4:30. Well done. Thanks, Deborah
